nodes.body: You are Wren, and today you mean to become a warden of the Thornwood, so when the Trial horn sounds long and low at the eaves of the enchanted forest, you are ready for it: one single day, dawn to dusk, to cross the whole wood and reach the far gate before the light dies. Four separate ways lead in. The old deer track runs quiet beneath the first thorns, the dry stream bed offers easier footing, the charcoal-burners road is wide and ancient, and the broken boundary wall lets you slip in behind the thorns. Choose your way in.

choices.label: Follow the old deer track.
Take the dry stream bed.
Walk the charcoal-burners road.
Slip through the broken boundary wall.

nodes.body: You take the old deer track, which winds quiet beneath the first thorns, soft underfoot and half-hidden, the kind of path that only the wood's own wary creatures ever bother to keep. It asks very little of you and gives you time to steady your breath and settle your nerve for the long day ahead. Then the thorns thicken and the track knots itself in with a dozen others, bringing you at last to the maze mouth, where every way into the Thornwood ties together. There is only one way onward from here.

choices.label: Press on to the maze mouth.

nodes.body: You drop down into the dry stream bed, whose pale stones make for easy footing, a clear road of gravel winding between the crowding thorns, and for a good while the going stays quick and simple. You make fine time and let yourself half-hope the whole day might run this smoothly. Then the bed climbs, the banks close in around you, and the gravel gives out at the same knot of thorn paths that swallows every other way in. The maze mouth waits ahead, so press on.

choices.label: Press on to the maze mouth.

nodes.body: You walk the charcoal-burners road, wide and old and deeply rutted by carts long since gone, and it is the boldest way in, out in the open beneath the grey sky, letting you stride along instead of creep. But every road within the Thornwood ends in the same place, and this ancient one proves no different at all. It runs straight and sure right up to the mouth of the bramble maze, where the easy walking finally stops for good. There is only one way forward now.

choices.label: Press on to the maze mouth.

nodes.body: You slip through the broken boundary wall, clambering over tumbled stone and in behind the outer thorns, cutting into the wood where few others would ever think to try. For a moment you feel genuinely clever, already past the worst of the hedge before the day has properly begun. Then the ground falls away beneath you and drops you down, from above, onto the maze mouth, that same knot of thorn paths where all the ways meet. Clever or not, the maze waits for everyone alike.

choices.label: Press on to the maze mouth.

nodes.body: The way simply pinches shut, and Bramble Maze closes to a narrow fist of thorn and darkness where you cannot find the marks again. You stand still and listen, but no horn answers you now. The Trial slips away for this season, leaving nothing but the long, cold walk home to try once more.

A single wrong footing sends the ground out from under you, and you slide down into a bramble-choked hollow with no handhold, its walls too smooth and steep to climb. Above you the light thins to grey, and the horns fade far off across the trees. You will not reach the gate tonight, and the Trial is lost.

You leave the true line for the easy-looking way, which for a dozen strides seems kinder underfoot. You promise yourself a return to the marks further on. Then the thorns lean closer and the scratched warden marks thin away to nothing, and an uneasy feeling settles over you. The ground behind has already changed its face, so the only way to learn where this leads is onward.

choices.label: Push on and hope it opens.

nodes.body: Deeper along the wrong way, Bramble Maze finally stops pretending to be kind, because the thorns lean closer and the scratched warden marks thin away to nothing, and the failing light turns every choice into a blind guess. You should have turned back a full bend ago, and you know it perfectly well now. But going back means facing all of it again in the dark. So you press further from the marks instead.

choices.label: Push on and hope it opens.

nodes.body: The cold works its way into you while the lost trail steadily wears away whatever strength remains, until your legs are finished and the light is nearly gone. Nothing clever is left to try. You settle against a thorn trunk, draw your knees up against the chill, and wait for the searchers who will come at first light. The Trial is over for this year.

ending.title: A Wrong Turn in the Brambles

nodes.body: You stand at the very heart of the Bramble Maze, where four thorn paths knot together and the warden marks are scratched deep on the trunks, pointing every wrong way and the single right one. The truest mark leads deeper into the tightest thorn, while a wider, gentler path opens invitingly to the side, far too kind to be the real test. Off through the briar a strange light glimmers, and a narrow gap offers a shortcut past it all. Read the marks, and choose your way.

choices.label: Take the true warden mark deeper into the Bramble Maze.
Take the wider, gentler way.
Turn aside toward the strange light.
Try the narrow shortcut around the test.

nodes.body: Your foot finds nothing where you were certain of solid ground, and you go down hard into a bramble-choked hollow whose sides crumble away at every desperate grip. The last of the day drains from the sky overhead while the far horns die into silence. You must wait now, for the crossing is finished for this year.

The wrong turning feels perfectly harmless at first, its branch wide and its footing soft. Then, quietly, the thorns lean closer and the scratched warden marks thin away to nothing. You have drifted clean off the warden signs without ever meaning to. Turning back now looks no clearer than pressing forward, so you keep to the strange path and hope it knows something you do not.

choices.label: Push on and hope it opens.

nodes.body: It only worsens the longer you follow it, for the thorns lean closer and the scratched warden marks thin away to nothing, and the deepening dusk drains away what little you could still make out. Some stubborn part of you keeps insisting the path must open up soon. The wiser part has already gone silent. You continue anyway, one careful step and then the next, deeper into the wrong of it.

choices.label: Push on and hope it opens.

nodes.body: Now there is no pretending left whatsoever, because the thorns lean closer and the scratched warden marks thin away to nothing, and the last of the marks lies far behind you in the dark. The trap of it is suddenly, coldly plain: this way was never once going to open. Yet your legs carry you onward regardless, because standing still in a place like this feels worse than moving. Just one more turn, you promise yourself.

choices.label: Push on and hope it opens.

nodes.body: You followed the mark in good faith, never guessing it was a false one, cut long ago by some other beaten hand to lead the tired and hopeful astray. It brings you only to a dead wall of bramble, with no way onward and no time left to turn back. A hard lesson learned too late: not every sign in this wood is a true one.

A single step off the marks, and Bramble Maze shifts around you, for the thorns lean closer and the scratched warden marks thin away to nothing. It felt like a clever shortcut a moment ago, but already it looks like a mistake in a shortcut's coat. Going back, though, would burn the last of the daylight. So you press on and trust to luck.

choices.label: Push on and hope it opens.

nodes.body: The excursion visibly tightens its grip on you now, since the thorns lean closer and the scratched warden marks thin away to nothing, until nothing remains to steer by except your own fading sense of the way. Each stride carries you further from the true trail. Each carries you nearer to whatever this path is truly leading toward. Against all better judgement, you refuse to stop.

choices.label: Push on and hope it opens.

nodes.body: Little by little the chill and the endless wandering use you up, and you have already crossed your own tracks twice now somewhere in Bramble Maze. Far better to stop than to blunder on blind through the dark. You sit down against a thorn trunk, cold to the bone, and wait for the morning and the search party, the gate still uncrossed behind you.

ending.title: A Wrong Turn in the Brambles

nodes.body: You work steadily deeper along the spine of Bramble Maze, where the warden marks run the hard, honest line into the tightening thorn. That remains the truest way, and equally the safest. Off to one side a strange light flickers, tempting in the gloom, but a light that does not belong in this wood is a warning rather than a welcome. A gentler easy path forks away to the left, and a broad warden run beckons past it all.

choices.label: Keep to the hardest, truest line.
Turn aside toward the strange light.
Double back and start over.
Trust the easy looking path.
Slip onto the wide warden run.

nodes.body: The sign you trusted so completely was a lie in the bark, an old cheat left by someone who failed here before you. It runs straight into a dead wall of bramble and stops dead, though you should have doubted it. Now the dusk is down and the gate lies beyond reach, so you turn for home wiser and a season poorer.

You leave the true line for the easy-looking way, which for a dozen strides seems kinder underfoot. You promise yourself a return to the marks further on. Then the briar closes overhead until the trunks lose every warden sign, and an uneasy feeling settles over you. The ground behind has already changed its face, so the only way to learn where this leads is onward.

choices.label: Push on and hope it opens.

choices.label: Push on and hope it opens.

nodes.body: You keep going long past the point where going on makes any sense, until the cold and the empty hours have finally taken the strength from your legs and the marks are lost beyond recovering. So you sit against a thorn trunk and let the dark settle over you. The searchers will find you tomorrow, and the Trial must wait another season.

No clever exit remains available to you. No warden mark survives to follow home. You keep moving because nothing else is left to do, on into whatever waits at the end of the wrong path.

choices.label: Push on and hope it opens.

nodes.body: You push far too hard and much too fast, and Bramble Maze catches you for it, driving you full into a snarl of thorn that refuses to let go and only draws tighter with every pull. By the time you finally work yourself loose, the dusk is long past and the horns have gone silent. Your own haste has cost you the Trial.

Further into Bramble Maze, the light is failing fast and the thorn closes in underfoot. The warden signs are still readable, if you take the time. But the dark comes on quickly, and part of you wants to rush the last of it, which is how the careless come to grief. An easy path and a straight cut both call to you, while a wide warden run offers a faster line.

choices.label: Read the signs and press ahead.
Rush it before the dark comes.
Trust the easy looking path.
Cut straight across the marks.
Slip onto the wide warden run.

nodes.body: There comes a point where pressing forward is only stubborn pride, and you reach it here, spent and shivering in the cold heart of Bramble Maze with the trail gone from beneath you. So you choose the sensible defeat: a sheltered place against a thorn trunk and a long wait for dawn. It is not brave, perhaps, but it keeps you alive for next year.

This path made its promise long ago, and now it means to keep it. You walk the last of it with your jaw set hard, bracing for the worst.

They simply raise one arm and point you back the way you came. Small things are the whole of the test, and the gate stays shut to you this year.

The spine of Bramble Maze narrows steadily, and your legs are beginning to tire beneath you. Hold your pace and read each mark in turn, however, and you will keep the trail. Doubt yourself now, though, and the temptation is to double back and start the whole stretch over, which quietly costs the daylight you cannot spare. Cutting across the marks or chancing a quicker line both look faster, and a warden run offers yet another way onward.

choices.label: Hold the pace and go on.
Double back and start over.
Cut straight across the marks.
Chance the quicker line.
Slip onto the wide warden run.

Long after dusk has passed you at last pull clear, spent and badly scratched, with nowhere left to go but home. The Trial is over.

There is no anger in it and no argument, only a quiet wave toward the way out. Here in Bramble Maze the Trial turns on details, and this one turned against you.

Somewhere far off, the last horn sounds without you. This season's crossing is finished before it ever reached the gate.

Deep within Bramble Maze now, the warden marks grow scarce and the thorn turns treacherous beneath you, yet following the signs onward with patience keeps the trail together. An easy-looking path opens invitingly to the side, smoother than the true way and therefore rightly suspect. Somewhere ahead a quicker line might save you time, while off through the dark a strange sound draws at you. Only one of these choices keeps you on the marks.

choices.label: Follow the warden marks onward.
Trust the easy looking path.
Chance the quicker line.
Follow the sound instead.

nodes.body: The test breaks on nothing large at all, only a beat spoken too soon or a reach made with the wrong hand, and that slight thing proves entirely sufficient. The warden nods once, not unkindly, and points you out of Bramble Maze toward the road home. You carry the hard little lesson away with you, meaning to try the whole crossing again next season.

You are nearing the far end of Bramble Maze now, with the next crossing close ahead of you. Keep to the hardest, truest line, and the warden marks will carry you cleanly through. But you are badly spent, and a gentler way curves temptingly off to the side, while a sound pulls at you from the dark and cutting straight across the marks looks like the short road home. Tired feet make poor choices, so hold steady and choose well.

choices.label: Keep to the hardest, truest line.
Cut straight across the marks.
Follow the sound instead.
Take the wider, gentler way.

nodes.body: A wide warden run opens off the main line, climbing steeply over root and deadfall, promising to eliminate a whole punishing corner of the Trial. It is a genuine path, and an ancient one, but a decidedly bold gamble. Hold the line and it saves you precious time; lose it and you are stranded nowhere. You take a steadying breath and commit yourself to the run.

choices.label: Follow the warden run onward.

nodes.body: The run holds true, but shadow pools thickly along it now, and the warden marks grow few and scattered. You are trusting the shape of the path far more than any actual sign. Still it seems to bend the right direction, over thorn and stone. You push onward through the gathering dark, watching for wherever it should rejoin the trail.

choices.label: Follow the warden run onward.

nodes.body: The side way twists sharply, and there ahead lies the spine of the true trail once more, closer than you had dared to hope. The run kept its word entirely. Even so, you weigh it a wary moment, half expecting one final trick of Bramble Maze. Then you step back onto the marked line and continue toward the crossing.

choices.label: Follow the warden run onward.

nodes.body: Here the warden marks split apart, and one branch becomes a broad run that hurdles over root and deadfall, straight toward wherever you most need to be. It could recover the daylight you keep steadily losing, provided your feet stay sure beneath you. It is time measured squarely against risk. Judging the time worth having, you start along the run through Bramble Maze.

choices.label: Follow the warden run onward.

nodes.body: Further along the excursion, the light gives out completely and the warden signs all but vanish beneath the thorn. Only the run itself still guides you now, a paler thread through the darkness of Bramble Maze. You could be a single stride from the true trail, or a full mile distant from it. You keep your nerve and keep moving.

choices.label: Follow the warden run onward.

nodes.body: At last the excursion swings back toward the spine of the Trial through Bramble Maze, and the warden marks are waiting plainly where the two paths finally rejoin. Do you trust it entirely, or turn aside just one more time? You have gambled quite enough for one stretch already. So you take the run home to the true trail and press on.

choices.label: Follow the warden run onward.

nodes.body: An old warden run breaks away from the spine at this point, striking over root and deadfall to spare you a long and punishing loop of the Trial. Bold walkers take it gladly, while careless ones are simply never seen again. You weigh the certain loss of light against the possible loss of the path. Then you set your feet to the run and go.

choices.label: Follow the warden run onward.

nodes.body: Deeper along the run, the shadows draw close and the marks dwindle away to almost nothing. The path itself is all you have left to steer by, and it demands that you trust it entirely blind. So you do exactly that, stepping careful over the thorn. You hope it holds its promise and carries you back to the spine of the trail.

choices.label: Follow the warden run onward.

nodes.body: The run bends around and, sure enough, delivers you back within clear sight of the warden marks. Some wary part of you still half expects a final trick of Bramble Maze. But the signs are genuinely true, cut clean and deep into the bark. So you leave the run behind and rejoin the trail with the daylight you managed to save.

choices.label: Follow the warden run onward.

choices.label: Follow the warden run onward.

nodes.body: Here the warden marks split apart, and one branch becomes a broad run that hurdles between the great elder roots, straight toward wherever you most need to be. It could recover the daylight you keep steadily losing, provided your feet stay sure beneath you. It is time measured squarely against risk. Judging the time worth having, you start along the run through Elder-Tree Council.

choices.label: Follow the warden run onward.

nodes.body: Further along the excursion, the light gives out completely and the warden signs all but vanish beneath the root. Only the run itself still guides you now, a paler thread through the darkness of Elder-Tree Council. You could be a single stride from the true trail, or a full mile distant from it. You keep your nerve and keep moving.

choices.label: Follow the warden run onward.

nodes.body: At last the excursion swings back toward the spine of the Trial through Elder-Tree Council, and the warden marks are waiting plainly where the two paths finally rejoin. Do you trust it entirely, or turn aside just one more time? You have gambled quite enough for one stretch already. So you take the run home to the true trail and press on.

choices.label: Follow the warden run onward.

nodes.body: An old warden run breaks away from the spine at this point, striking between the great elder roots to spare you a long and punishing loop of the Trial. Bold walkers take it gladly, while careless ones are simply never seen again. You weigh the certain loss of light against the possible loss of the path. Then you set your feet to the run and go.

choices.label: Follow the warden run onward.

nodes.body: Deeper along the run, the shadows draw close and the marks dwindle away to almost nothing. The path itself is all you have left to steer by, and it demands that you trust it entirely blind. So you do exactly that, stepping careful over the root. You hope it holds its promise and carries you back to the spine of the trail.

choices.label: Follow the warden run onward.

nodes.body: The run bends around and, sure enough, delivers you back within clear sight of the warden marks. Some wary part of you still half expects a final trick of Elder-Tree Council. But the signs are genuinely true, cut clean and deep into the bark. So you leave the run behind and rejoin the trail with the daylight you managed to save.

choices.label: Follow the warden run onward.

nodes.body: You push far too hard and much too fast, and Far Ridge catches you for it, driving you full into a snarl of stone that refuses to let go and only draws tighter with every pull. By the time you finally work yourself loose, the dusk is long past and the horns have gone silent. Your own haste has cost you the Trial.

It is a small thing that finally fails you in Far Ridge, a rushed word or a hand offered wrong or a single step taken out of turn, and the warden watching does not scold you for it. They simply raise one arm and point you back the way you came. Small things are the whole of the test, and the gate stays shut to you this year.

You leave the true line for the easy-looking way, which for a dozen strides seems kinder underfoot. You promise yourself a return to the marks further on. Then the ledge narrows over the drop until the guiding cairns give out beneath you, and an uneasy feeling settles over you. The ground behind has already changed its face, so the only way to learn where this leads is onward.

choices.label: Push on and hope it opens.

nodes.body: Deeper along the wrong way, Far Ridge finally stops pretending to be kind, because the ledge narrows over the drop until the guiding cairns give out beneath you, and the failing light turns every choice into a blind guess. You should have turned back a full bend ago, and you know it perfectly well now. But going back means facing all of it again in the dark. So you press further from the marks instead.

choices.label: Push on and hope it opens.

nodes.body: A single wrong footing sends the ground out from under you, and you slide down into a crevice in the rock with no handhold, its walls too smooth and steep to climb. Above you the light thins to grey, and the horns fade far off across the trees. You will not reach the gate tonight, and the Trial is lost.

ending.title: A Wrong Turn in the Ridge

nodes.body: The Far Ridge climbs toward the last of the light, where the third warden test, a test of raw nerve, is strung along a wind-scoured spine of rock high above the whole darkening forest. The truest mark keeps to the high, hard line, while a wider, gentler path drops away below the wind, safer-seeming and quietly wrong. A strange light burns far out on the rock, and a narrow ledge offers a shortcut across. Hold your nerve, and choose.

choices.label: Take the true warden mark deeper into the Far Ridge.
Take the wider, gentler way.
Turn aside toward the strange light.
Try the narrow shortcut around the test.

nodes.body: You do nearly everything right, and yet the one wrong move is enough; a hasty answer, a careless foot, and the warden's face closes like a shut door. There is no anger in it and no argument, only a quiet wave toward the way out. Here in Far Ridge the Trial turns on details, and this one turned against you.

The wrong turning feels perfectly harmless at first, its branch wide and its footing soft. Then, quietly, the ledge narrows over the drop until the guiding cairns give out beneath you. You have drifted clean off the warden signs without ever meaning to. Turning back now looks no clearer than pressing forward, so you keep to the strange path and hope it knows something you do not.

choices.label: Push on and hope it opens.

nodes.body: It only worsens the longer you follow it, for the ledge narrows over the drop until the guiding cairns give out beneath you, and the deepening dusk drains away what little you could still make out. Some stubborn part of you keeps insisting the path must open up soon. The wiser part has already gone silent. You continue anyway, one careful step and then the next, deeper into the wrong of it.

choices.label: Push on and hope it opens.

Just one more turn, you promise yourself.

A single step off the marks, and Far Ridge shifts around you, for the ledge narrows over the drop until the guiding cairns give out beneath you. It felt like a clever shortcut a moment ago, but already it looks like a mistake in a shortcut's coat. Going back, though, would burn the last of the daylight. So you press on and trust to luck.

choices.label: Push on and hope it opens.

Each stride carries you further from the true trail. Each carries you nearer to whatever this path is truly leading toward. Against all better judgement, you refuse to stop.

choices.label: Push on and hope it opens.

A gentler easy path forks away to the left, and a broad warden run beckons past it all.

choices.label: Keep to the hardest, truest line.
Turn aside toward the strange light.
Double back and start over.
Trust the easy looking path.
Slip onto the wide warden run.

nodes.body: Your foot finds nothing where you were certain of solid ground, and you go down hard into a pit below the outer wall whose sides crumble away at every desperate grip. The last of the day drains from the sky overhead while the far horns die into silence. You must wait now, for the crossing is finished for this year.

You leave the true line for the easy-looking way, which for a dozen strides seems kinder underfoot. You promise yourself a return to the marks further on. Then the last red light finally fails and every gate sign vanishes into the dark, and an uneasy feeling settles over you. The ground behind has already changed its face, so the only way to learn where this leads is onward.

choices.label: Push on and hope it opens.

nodes.body: Deeper along the wrong way, Far Gate finally stops pretending to be kind, because the last red light finally fails and every gate sign vanishes into the dark, and the failing light turns every choice into a blind guess. You should have turned back a full bend ago, and you know it perfectly well now. But going back means facing all of it again in the dark. So you press further from the marks instead.

choices.label: Push on and hope it opens.

nodes.body: Too late you understand that the mark was wrong the whole way along, weathered and recut by some past failure until it looked true. It ends without warning at a blind wall of fallen gate-stone, with nothing at all beyond it. You read exactly what you wished to read, and the Trial is lost on a false sign you will not soon forget.

ending.title: A Wrong Turn in the Gate

nodes.body: The wrong turning feels perfectly harmless at first, its branch wide and its footing soft. Then, quietly, the last red light finally fails and every gate sign vanishes into the dark. You have drifted clean off the warden signs without ever meaning to. Turning back now looks no clearer than pressing forward, so you keep to the strange path and hope it knows something you do not.

choices.label: Push on and hope it opens.

nodes.body: It only worsens the longer you follow it, for the last red light finally fails and every gate sign vanishes into the dark, and the deepening dusk drains away what little you could still make out. Some stubborn part of you keeps insisting the path must open up soon. The wiser part has already gone silent. You continue anyway, one careful step and then the next, deeper into the wrong of it.

choices.label: Push on and hope it opens.

nodes.body: The way has you now, well and truly, because the cut stone gives way to loose rubble and the guiding marks run out completely, and you understand far too late that it was only ever leading toward a single place. No clever exit remains available to you. No warden mark survives to follow home. You keep moving because nothing else is left to do, on into whatever waits at the end of the wrong path.

choices.label: Push on and hope it opens.

nodes.body: The cold works its way into you while the lost trail steadily wears away whatever strength remains, until your legs are finished and the light is nearly gone. Nothing clever is left to try. You settle in the cold grass beneath the wall, draw your knees up against the chill, and wait for the searchers who will come at first light. The Trial is over for this year.

Further into Far Gate, the light is failing fast and the cut stone closes in underfoot. The warden signs are still readable, if you take the time. But the dark comes on quickly, and part of you wants to rush the last of it, which is how the careless come to grief. An easy path and a straight cut both call to you, while a wide warden run offers a faster line.

choices.label: Read the signs and press ahead.
Rush it before the dark comes.
Trust the easy looking path.
Cut straight across the marks.
Slip onto the wide warden run.

nodes.body: Somewhere in Far Gate, long before tonight, a beaten hand carved a false mark into the bark, and tonight you are the one who chose to believe it. It leads you faithfully to a blind wall of fallen gate-stone and abandons you there without apology. Trust, it turns out, is its own kind of trap. The horns sound far away, and you have failed on a stranger's old lie.

ending.title: A Wrong Turn in the Gate

nodes.body: A single step off the marks, and Far Gate shifts around you, for the last red light finally fails and every gate sign vanishes into the dark. It felt like a clever shortcut a moment ago, but already it looks like a mistake in a shortcut's coat. Going back, though, would burn the last of the daylight. So you press on and trust to luck.

choices.label: Push on and hope it opens.

nodes.body: The excursion visibly tightens its grip on you now, since the last red light finally fails and every gate sign vanishes into the dark, until nothing remains to steer by except your own fading sense of the way. Each stride carries you further from the true trail. Each carries you nearer to whatever this path is truly leading toward. Against all better judgement, you refuse to stop.

choices.label: Push on and hope it opens.

nodes.body: You are committed now, whether you like it or not, since the cut stone gives way to loose rubble and the guiding marks run out completely and the dark has swallowed the trail back. This path made its promise long ago, and now it means to keep it. You walk the last of it with your jaw set hard, bracing for the worst.

choices.label: Push on and hope it opens.

nodes.body: Little by little the chill and the endless wandering use you up, and you have already crossed your own tracks twice now somewhere in Far Gate. Far better to stop than to blunder on blind through the dark. You sit down in the cold grass beneath the wall, cold to the bone, and wait for the morning and the search party, the gate still uncrossed behind you.

You leave the true line for the easy-looking way, which for a dozen strides seems kinder underfoot. You promise yourself a return to the marks further on. Then the outer wall crumbles away into bramble until the marked way is wholly lost, and an uneasy feeling settles over you. The ground behind has already changed its face, so the only way to learn where this leads is onward.

choices.label: Push on and hope it opens.

You should have turned back a full bend ago, and you know it perfectly well now. But going back means facing all of it again in the dark. So you press further from the marks instead.

choices.label: Push on and hope it opens.

nodes.body: You push far too hard and much too fast, and Far Gate catches you for it, driving you full into a snarl of cut stone that refuses to let go and only draws tighter with every pull. By the time you finally work yourself loose, the dusk is long past and the horns have gone silent. Your own haste has cost you the Trial.

The spine of Far Gate narrows steadily, and your legs are beginning to tire beneath you. Hold your pace and read each mark in turn, however, and you will keep the trail. Doubt yourself now, though, and the temptation is to double back and start the whole stretch over, which quietly costs the daylight you cannot spare. Cutting across the marks or chancing a quicker line both look faster, and a warden run offers yet another way onward.

choices.label: Hold the pace and go on.
Double back and start over.
Cut straight across the marks.
Chance the quicker line.
Slip onto the wide warden run.

nodes.body: You keep going long past the point where going on makes any sense, until the cold and the empty hours have finally taken the strength from your legs and the marks are lost beyond recovering. So you sit in the cold grass beneath the wall and let the dark settle over you. The searchers will find you tomorrow, and the Trial must wait another season.

The wrong turning feels perfectly harmless at first, its branch wide and its footing soft. Then, quietly, the outer wall crumbles away into bramble until the marked way is wholly lost. You have drifted clean off the warden signs without ever meaning to. Turning back now looks no clearer than pressing forward, so you keep to the strange path and hope it knows something you do not.

Some stubborn part of you keeps insisting the path must open up soon. The wiser part has already gone silent. You continue anyway, one careful step and then the next, deeper into the wrong of it.

choices.label: Push on and hope it opens.

nodes.body: Speed makes you careless, and in Far Gate carelessness has consequences; you rush a bad stretch and drive yourself straight into a tangle that holds you completely fast. It takes hours of slow, patient work to get free, and by then the light is gone and the gate stands shut. You beat yourself tonight, not the wood.

A single step off the marks, and Far Gate shifts around you, for the outer wall crumbles away into bramble until the marked way is wholly lost. It felt like a clever shortcut a moment ago, but already it looks like a mistake in a shortcut's coat. Going back, though, would burn the last of the daylight. So you press on and trust to luck.

Each stride carries you further from the true trail. Each carries you nearer to whatever this path is truly leading toward. Against all better judgement, you refuse to stop.

choices.label: Push on and hope it opens.

nodes.body: Now there is no pretending left whatsoever, because the last red light finally fails and every gate sign vanishes into the dark, and the last of the marks lies far behind you in the dark. The trap of it is suddenly, coldly plain: this way was never once going to open. Yet your legs carry you onward regardless, because standing still in a place like this feels worse than moving. Just one more turn, you promise yourself.

choices.label: Push on and hope it opens.

nodes.body: You do nearly everything right, and yet the one wrong move is enough; a hasty answer, a careless foot, and the warden's face closes like a shut door. There is no anger in it and no argument, only a quiet wave toward the way out. Here in Far Gate the Trial turns on details, and this one turned against you.

Deep within Far Gate now, the warden marks grow scarce and the cut stone turns treacherous beneath you, yet following the signs onward with patience keeps the trail together. An easy-looking path opens invitingly to the side, smoother than the true way and therefore rightly suspect. Somewhere ahead a quicker line might save you time, while off through the dark a strange sound draws at you. Only one of these choices keeps you on the marks.

choices.label: Follow the warden marks onward.
Trust the easy looking path.
Chance the quicker line.
Follow the sound instead.

nodes.body: Trying desperately to win back lost time, you crash onward without reading the ground, and the cut stone snares you at once, gripping harder the harder you fight against it. Long after dusk has passed you at last pull clear, spent and badly scratched, with nowhere left to go but home. The Trial is over.

You leave the true line for the easy-looking way, which for a dozen strides seems kinder underfoot. You promise yourself a return to the marks further on. Then the sounding horns pull you off the marked line and away into the gathering dark, and an uneasy feeling settles over you. The ground behind has already changed its face, so the only way to learn where this leads is onward.

choices.label: Push on and hope it opens.

You should have turned back a full bend ago, and you know it perfectly well now. But going back means facing all of it again in the dark. So you press further from the marks instead.

No clever exit remains available to you. No warden mark survives to follow home. You keep moving because nothing else is left to do, on into whatever waits at the end of the wrong path.

choices.label: Push on and hope it opens.

nodes.body: The test breaks on nothing large at all, only a beat spoken too soon or a reach made with the wrong hand, and that slight thing proves entirely sufficient. The warden nods once, not unkindly, and points you out of Far Gate toward the road home. You carry the hard little lesson away with you, meaning to try the whole crossing again next season.

The wrong turning feels perfectly harmless at first, its branch wide and its footing soft. Then, quietly, the sounding horns pull you off the marked line and away into the gathering dark. You have drifted clean off the warden signs without ever meaning to. Turning back now looks no clearer than pressing forward, so you keep to the strange path and hope it knows something you do not.

choices.label: Push on and hope it opens.

nodes.body: It only worsens the longer you follow it, for the sounding horns pull you off the marked line and away into the gathering dark, and the deepening dusk drains away what little you could still make out. Some stubborn part of you keeps insisting the path must open up soon. The wiser part has already gone silent. You continue anyway, one careful step and then the next, deeper into the wrong of it.

choices.label: Push on and hope it opens.

nodes.body: The gap you chose shrinks steadily to nothing, and the dark and the cut stone press in from every side without a single warden sign to show a way through. You turn, and turn again, and find only more of the same closing wood. The Trial ends here, unfinished; next season you will read the marks better.

You are nearing the far end of Far Gate now, with the next crossing close ahead of you. Keep to the hardest, truest line, and the warden marks will carry you cleanly through. But you are badly spent, and a gentler way curves temptingly off to the side, while a sound pulls at you from the dark and cutting straight across the marks looks like the short road home. Tired feet make poor choices, so hold steady and choose well.

choices.label: Keep to the hardest, truest line.
Cut straight across the marks.
Follow the sound instead.
Take the wider, gentler way.

nodes.body: A wide warden run opens off the main line, climbing steeply beneath the crumbling outer wall, promising to eliminate a whole punishing corner of the Trial. It is a genuine path, and an ancient one, but a decidedly bold gamble. Hold the line and it saves you precious time; lose it and you are stranded nowhere. You take a steadying breath and commit yourself to the run.

choices.label: Follow the warden run onward.

nodes.body: The run holds true, but shadow pools thickly along it now, and the warden marks grow few and scattered. You are trusting the shape of the path far more than any actual sign. Still it seems to bend the right direction, over cut stone and stone. You push onward through the gathering dark, watching for wherever it should rejoin the trail.

choices.label: Follow the warden run onward.

nodes.body: The side way twists sharply, and there ahead lies the spine of the true trail once more, closer than you had dared to hope. The run kept its word entirely. Even so, you weigh it a wary moment, half expecting one final trick of Far Gate. Then you step back onto the marked line and continue toward the crossing.

choices.label: Follow the warden run onward.

nodes.body: Here the warden marks split apart, and one branch becomes a broad run that hurdles beneath the crumbling outer wall, straight toward wherever you most need to be. It could recover the daylight you keep steadily losing, provided your feet stay sure beneath you. It is time measured squarely against risk. Judging the time worth having, you start along the run through Far Gate.

choices.label: Follow the warden run onward.

nodes.body: Further along the excursion, the light gives out completely and the warden signs all but vanish beneath the cut stone. Only the run itself still guides you now, a paler thread through the darkness of Far Gate. You could be a single stride from the true trail, or a full mile distant from it. You keep your nerve and keep moving.

choices.label: Follow the warden run onward.

nodes.body: At last the excursion swings back toward the spine of the Trial through Far Gate, and the warden marks are waiting plainly where the two paths finally rejoin. Do you trust it entirely, or turn aside just one more time? You have gambled quite enough for one stretch already. So you take the run home to the true trail and press on.

choices.label: Follow the warden run onward.

nodes.body: An old warden run breaks away from the spine at this point, striking beneath the crumbling outer wall to spare you a long and punishing loop of the Trial. Bold walkers take it gladly, while careless ones are simply never seen again. You weigh the certain loss of light against the possible loss of the path. Then you set your feet to the run and go.

choices.label: Follow the warden run onward.

nodes.body: Deeper along the run, the shadows draw close and the marks dwindle away to almost nothing. The path itself is all you have left to steer by, and it demands that you trust it entirely blind. So you do exactly that, stepping careful over the cut stone. You hope it holds its promise and carries you back to the spine of the trail.

choices.label: Follow the warden run onward.

nodes.body: The run bends around and, sure enough, delivers you back within clear sight of the warden marks. Some wary part of you still half expects a final trick of Far Gate. But the signs are genuinely true, cut clean and deep into the bark. So you leave the run behind and rejoin the trail with the daylight you managed to save.

choices.label: Follow the warden run onward.

nodes.body: You spill out of the trees before the Far Gate just as the last horn dies away, and the warden bar is still drawn while three wardens stand in the gateway, waiting to judge how you ran the whole of the Trial. There is no hiding whatever the long day has cost you. You can present yourself openly and ask to be named, or beg to cross instead by the wild ways. You could try to slip the gate unseen in the dark, though wardens miss nothing, or you could sink down, utterly spent, and simply let the horns pass you by. Choose.

choices.label: Present yourself openly and ask to be named.
Beg to pass by the wild ways instead.
Try to slip the gate unseen in the dark.
Sink down, spent, and let the horns pass.

nodes.body: The head warden lifts a hand for quiet and asks you to account fully for the day, every mark you read and every creature you met and every test you faced, and then comes the real question: name the single thing that carried you the whole way through the Thornwood. The wardens watch your face far more closely than they listen to your actual words. You can answer that it was steadiness and the marks, or that it was mercy shown to the beasts, or you can tell the plain truth and admit that it was luck. Speak, then.

choices.label: Answer that it was steadiness and the marks.
Answer that it was mercy shown to the beasts.
Answer that it was luck, and say so plainly.

nodes.body: The wardens will not draw the great bar for a latecomer, since that gate is for those who arrive with the light still in the sky. But one warden studies you a long moment and quietly offers another road. The wild ways: a harder crossing, walked alone through the dark, that earns a lesser but real place among the outer wardens if it is walked well. You can take the wild ways and earn that place tonight, or refuse and wait to try the whole Trial again next season. Decide.

choices.label: Walk the wild ways and earn the lesser place.
Refuse, and wait for next season instead.

nodes.body: The head warden nods, slow and clearly satisfied, and lays the green cord across your shoulders. "Steadiness, and the marks," she says. "That is a warden's answer." The heavy bar lifts at last, the whole test passed, and the gate swings wide open onto the lit hall beyond, warm and loud with waiting wardens. You have crossed the entire Thornwood in a single day, and you are named among them now.

ending.title: Named a Warden of the Thornwood

nodes.body: At the word mercy the three wardens exchange a long look, and something in them visibly settles. "To spare the warren beasts when you might so easily have struck at them," the eldest says gravely, "is the truest warden heart of all." They name you a warden of the wild ways, with genuine honour, and swing the gate open. Of every answer you might have given, that was the one they had quietly hoped to hear.

You tell them plainly that it was luck, more than anything else, that carried you through to the gate, and for a long moment there is only silence. Then the wardens laugh, not unkindly, at so honest an answer to so grand a question. "Luck," the head warden says warmly, "and the wit to admit it." They name you a warden all the same, for honesty is the very first mark they look for.

ending.title: Named for a Plain True Answer

nodes.body: You take the wild ways, and all through the dark you walk them entirely alone, cold and footsore, reading the faint marks by feel as much as by sight, and more than once you very nearly turn back. But you do not turn back. At dawn you come out on the far side, utterly spent yet still standing, and the outer wardens give you a real place among their number. It is a hard crossing, and it is well and truly won.

You wait for the dark to thicken, then move for the gate low and quiet, hoping to slip the drawn bar unseen. But the wardens catch you the moment the shadow shifts, for they have watched this gate a hundred years. There is no anger in them, only a slow shake of the head, because a Trial ended by sneaking is no true pass. They turn you around for the long walk back out, and the season is lost.

You have absolutely nothing left in you, so you sink down into the cold grass before the gate and simply let the last horn die away around you. The heavy bar does not lift. The wardens wait a while longer, then look away, and in that moment you understand that it is over. So very near, and yet still short of it. The Trial is lost for this year, to be attempted again when the season turns and the horns sound once more.

You will not take a lesser crossing on tired and shaking legs, not even for a real place among the wardens, because it seems far better to walk the whole Trial again, whole and proud, than to slip in by the side door tonight. So you thank the watching warden, turn away from the gate, and start the long road home. Next season you will read the marks better and cross by the true gate. This is not defeat; it is a promise.
